question: For the live similarity-search component, what reasoning favored the incremental-insert graph design over the cluster-rebuild layout, and what cost triggered it?
gold_answer: The graph-navigation index was chosen over the cluster/centroid (IVF) approach because rebuild cost spiked 3x at the 50M-vector shard boundary and re-clustering on corpus growth is a non-starter for a live index, whereas the graph approach supports incremental inserts.
evidence_prose: 2024-02-14 · Platform design review — Dmitri: 'IVF rebuild cost spiked 3x at the 50M-vector shard boundary during load test; switched index backend to the graph-nav approach. Re-clustering on growth is a non-starter for a live index.' Filed under ADR-07.

question: How much did a two-stage scoring approach improve ranked-result relevance compared with using one model to fetch candidates?
gold_answer: Cross-encoder reranking raised nDCG@10 by 0.09 over bi-encoder-only
evidence_prose: 2024-03-28 | Reranker RFC outcome. Ran the standard eval suite on 2,400 judged queries. Second-pass scoring with the cross-encoder moved nDCG@10 from 0.61 to 0.70; latency cost is 38ms median which Growth says is acceptable. Shipping to prod gated on billing-tier check.

question: What was the storage saving from a lower-precision representation, and did answer quality drop noticeably?
gold_answer: int8 quantization cut memory 3.8x with under 1% recall loss at k=10
evidence_prose: 2024-05-06 | Platform team design doc — vector compression options. Scalar quantization at 8-bit yielded a 3.8× footprint reduction on the 50M-embedding shard. Recall@10 delta was -0.7% against fp32 baseline — within the acceptable band Elena set in the SLA. Approved for GA rollout.

question: On rare or unusual searches, how did mixing keyword and semantic matching compare to a purely neural approach?
gold_answer: BM25 + dense fusion beat dense-only by 11 MRR@10 points on long-tail queries
evidence_prose: 2024-06-19 | Query router post-experiment notes. Tail-query slice (bottom 15% by monthly frequency) was where dense alone hurt us most. Reciprocal-rank fusion against the keyword scorer closed the gap — MRR@10 climbed 11 points on that slice. Ana flagged that head queries actually regressed 1.4 points; router now segments by query frequency before fusing.

question: What efficiency gain came from processing many items at once during the encoding stage rather than one request at a time?
gold_answer: Batch size 64 cut per-document GPU cost 2.3x compared to one-at-a-time inference
evidence_prose: 2024-07-11 | Embedding service perf run. Compared individual encode calls against bucketed micro-batches on the A10G fleet. At bucket depth 64 we hit 2.3× the tokens-per-dollar of the singleton path. Carla updating autoscaler targets; anything above 128 shows diminishing returns due to padding overhead.

question: Did changing the scoring math used during candidate lookup reorder the results, and was anything faster?
gold_answer: Dot-product on normalized vectors cut query latency 18% with no ranking change versus cosine
evidence_prose: 2024-08-22 | Search team RFC — similarity kernel audit. When all vectors are unit-normalized at ingest time, cosine and inner-product rank identically. We benchmarked the kernel swap on 1k QPS replay: wall-clock per query fell 18% because the dot path avoids the magnitude division. No diff in eval metrics. Merging to main this sprint.

question: How did adding records to the store on the fly stack up against periodic from-scratch builds on both result quality and turnaround time?
gold_answer: Incremental upsert kept 98% of recall vs full rebuild and was 6x faster to complete
evidence_prose: 2024-09-30 | On-call postmortem follow-up. Root cause of last week's 4-hour index freeze was the nightly full rebuild blocking query traffic. Tested streaming upsert on the staging shard for two weeks: recall held at 98% of the rebuild baseline and the re-index window shrank from 3h to 28 min. Ben proposing we retire the nightly job entirely by Q1.

question: What was the retrieval gain from training the model on the in-house corpus versus shipping a generic off-the-shelf release?
gold_answer: Domain fine-tuning improved recall@5 by 14 percentage points over the base checkpoint
evidence_prose: 2024-11-14 | Search team experiment log — custom encoder v1. Ran contrastive fine-tuning on 220k HELIX query-passage pairs for 3 epochs. Recall@5 on our internal dev set jumped from 61% to 75% compared to the public checkpoint we were shipping. Dmitri says latency is identical; serving infra unchanged. Promoting to shadow traffic next week.

question: How much did storing precomputed vectors for the most-run searches lighten the load on the encoding service?
gold_answer: Embedding cache for top-1000 queries cut embedding service load by 34%
evidence_prose: 2025-01-09 | Platform standup. Elena shipped the query embedding cache to prod yesterday. Hot-path hit rate settled at 34% of total encode volume eliminated — that's roughly 340 GPU-minutes per hour reclaimed at peak. Staleness was a concern but the 1k stable head barely changes week-over-week; we'll re-seed Sundays.

question: What speed gain came from giving each account its own slice of data instead of pooling everyone together?
gold_answer: Per-tenant sharding lowered P95 query latency 27% compared to the shared index
evidence_prose: 2025-02-27 | Growth / Platform joint design review. The noisy-neighbor problem on the shared index was confirmed in the Q4 postmortem. We ran a 6-week shadow experiment with isolated per-customer segments on a representative set of enterprise accounts. P95 fell from 410ms to 300ms. Ana signing off on the migration plan; rollout starts with accounts >1M docs.

question: Did using a thinned-out computation pattern in the second-pass model cost any accuracy, and how did its speed change?
gold_answer: Sparse attention in the reranker matched dense quality and was 2.1x faster
evidence_prose: 2025-04-03 | Reranker v3 experiment summary. Tried local-window + learned top-k routing instead of full quadratic attention. On the Q1 eval set the nDCG@10 delta was -0.002 — effectively noise. Throughput at the 95th latency percentile improved by 2.1×. Carla flagging this as the path to enabling reranking on the Starter billing tier without blowing the cost model.

question: What coverage improvement came from automatically rewriting what users typed before fetching, and what speed penalty did it carry?
gold_answer: Learned query expansion boosted recall@20 by 8 points at a cost of 55ms added median latency
evidence_prose: 2025-05-20 | Query router experiment debrief — expansion module. The T5-small reformulation step added a 55ms tail to median query time but the coverage numbers were hard to ignore: recall@20 moved from 72% to 80% on the enterprise benchmark set. Ben pushing to make expansion an opt-in feature flag per tenant so latency-sensitive customers can skip it; Growth wants it on by default for the new Insight tier.

question: What language was the document intake service originally written in before the team switched runtimes due to resource exhaustion under load?
gold_answer: Python
evidence_prose: 2024-03-28 — Post-mortem P1-24-007. Root cause: CPython GC pauses and per-object overhead drove RSS past container limits during the 2 AM batch window; worker pods OOM-killed 47 times. Resolution: Platform greenlit a Go rewrite; Python service deprecated end of Q2'24.

question: Which third-party vector database did the Search team trial and ultimately turn down before building their own persistence layer?
gold_answer: Weaviate
evidence_prose: 2024-01-17 — Design doc HELIX-DD-003 addendum. Elena: Weaviate PoC completed; cluster management overhead nontrivial, and BSL license creates uncertainty for our SaaS distribution model. Recommendation: discard external vendor path, own the persistence layer.

question: What processing model for generating vector representations was replaced because it caused the document intake pipeline to stall during a load spike?
gold_answer: Synchronous (inline) embedding during ingestion; replaced with an asynchronous queue-based embedding architecture.
evidence_prose: 2024-07-11 — Platform incident review INC-0441. Ingest endpoint p99 timeout rate hit 12% during the enterprise onboarding spike; embedding service saturated inline. Resolution shipped same day: decouple via Kafka topic, embed asynchronously, surface completion via webhook.

question: Which query language did the team trial for the customer-facing console backend before reverting to a more conventional interface style?
gold_answer: GraphQL
evidence_prose: 2024-08-05 — Dashboard RFC-19 decision log. Elena: GraphQL PoC ran six weeks; frontend iteration velocity noticeably slower — schema churn, n+1 resolver issues, tooling overhead. Reverting to REST with OpenAPI spec; ship the v2 endpoints by end of sprint 34.

question: Which augmentation technique that synthesizes fictitious relevant passages was prototyped and then dropped from the production serving path?
gold_answer: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings / LLM-generated hypothetical answers for query expansion)
evidence_prose: 2025-01-09 — Query router RFC-27 closeout. Dmitri: HyDE offline evals showed +4 nDCG on long-tail queries, but the extra LLM call added 300–500ms and ~$0.003 per query at our volume — burns the margin on Starter tier. Carla: tabling it; revisit if we ever gate it to Enterprise only.

question: Which managed versus self-hosted tradeoff drove the selection of the primary embedding storage backend?
gold_answer: Qdrant was chosen over Pinecone because self-hosting requirements and cost predictability at scale favored it.
evidence_prose: 2024-02-14 — Platform sync: Ben ran a 3-way bake-off (Weaviate / our shortlisted managed option / Qdrant). Pinecone's per-vector pricing blew up the Q3 cost model past $40k/mo at 2B vectors; Weaviate's Rust port wasn't stable enough for prod. Went with Qdrant on bare metal — gives us the knobs we need and the bill stays flat regardless of query volume.

question: What library and pretrained checkpoint did the team standardize on for converting text to dense vectors?
gold_answer: sentence-transformers with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 was standardized as the default encoder.
evidence_prose: 2024-03-07 — Carla's embedding spike wrap-up: tried instructor-xl and e5-large-v2 first — recall was marginally better but p99 blew past our 20ms SLO. Landed on the small MiniLM checkpoint via the sentence-transformers library; 6ms median on the g4dn.xlarge fleet, NDCG@10 within 3 points of the big models on our internal eval set. Shipping it.

question: Why did the team buy an outside service for reordering search hits rather than operating their own ranking model in-house?
gold_answer: Cohere Rerank API was chosen to avoid the overhead of managing a self-hosted GPU fleet for cross-encoder inference.
evidence_prose: 2024-05-22 — Reranker RFC outcome (RFC-031, authors: Ana, Dmitri): proposal to self-host a cross-encoder was tabled. Search team doesn't want to own another GPU autoscaling group — we're not an ML infra shop. Cohere's rerank endpoint is sub-100ms and the pricing at our p50 query volume is negligible vs. engineer-weeks of k8s tuning. Decision: vendor API, revisit if monthly bill exceeds $8k.

question: What durable queue was placed between the page-fetching stage and the components that compute dense representations, and why over the alternative?
gold_answer: Apache Kafka was chosen as the durable event stream between the document crawler and embedding workers, because it preserves replay semantics that SQS lacked.
evidence_prose: 2024-01-19 — Platform standup notes: Ben and Elena debated SQS vs. Kafka for the ingest fan-out. SQS would be fine for current load but we'd lose replay semantics — post-mortems from Q4'23 showed replay was critical when the embedding workers crashed mid-batch. Kafka on MSK it is; retention set to 72h. Topic: helix.documents.raw.

question: What persistence solution did the team choose for recording per-request timing metrics instead of a purpose-built observability store?
gold_answer: TimescaleDB (a PostgreSQL extension) was chosen for query-level latency telemetry instead of a dedicated time-series database.
evidence_prose: 2024-06-10 — SRE runbook update (Dmitri): added Timescale hypertable retention policy (90 days). Background: Growth wanted InfluxDB; Platform pushed back — we already run RDS Postgres for billing, adding another datastore just for telemetry doesn't clear the ops-burden bar. Timescale extension on a separate schema gives us time-bucketing and continuous aggregates without a new fleet.

question: Which framework powers the testing harness, and what made it preferable over options needing hand-labeled ground truth?
gold_answer: Ragas was chosen for the eval harness because it computes faithfulness and answer relevancy without requiring gold labels.
evidence_prose: 2024-08-03 — Eval harness design doc v2 (Ana): considered BEIR + custom LLM-judge pipeline, but standing up gold-label annotation takes weeks we don't have pre-Q3 launch. Ragas scores faithfulness and context precision reference-free using our existing LLM endpoint. Wired into CI — any PR touching retrieval logic must show delta vs. baseline on the internal 500-query seed set.

question: Which UI framework did the customer-facing console adopt, and what fetch-pattern fit pushed the team off the more popular option?
gold_answer: Remix was chosen over Next.js because its nested data-loading model better matched the server-side query patterns needed by the web dashboard.
evidence_prose: 2024-04-29 — Growth eng weekly: Carla demoed the Next.js spike — RSC is still too unstable for our needs and the nested loader pattern in Remix maps cleanly onto our multi-tenant query structure (customer → project → index → stats, each needing its own fetch). Team voted Remix. Tooling: pnpm workspace, Tailwind, deployed on Railway behind Cloudflare.

question: What outside payments platform handles subscription and consumption charges, and what risk concern steered the team away from building it themselves?
gold_answer: Stripe with usage-based metering was chosen over a homegrown invoicing system primarily to reduce compliance (PCI/revenue recognition) surface area.
evidence_prose: 2024-01-31 — Billing RFC-009 outcome: proposal to build invoicing in-house was rejected. Elena flagged that homegrown billing means owning PCI scope, revenue recognition edge cases, and dunning logic — none of which maps to our core competency. Stripe Billing with meter events wired to our query-count telemetry. Revenue leakage test: <0.3% on the staging load replay.

question: What shared data store was introduced to make request throttling survive rollouts, and which structure inside it backs the per-customer caps?
gold_answer: Redis sorted sets were adopted for per-tenant rate limiting after the in-process token bucket was found to lose state on pod restarts.
evidence_prose: 2024-07-15 — Query router post-mortem PM-014: root cause was the in-process limiter losing its window state on every rollout, letting burst traffic hammer Qdrant. Fix shipped 2024-07-18: sliding-window counter backed by Redis sorted sets, TTL-keyed per tenant. No state lost across restarts. Ops runbook updated; Ben owns the Redis ElastiCache cluster sizing.

question: Which open instrumentation standard did the team migrate to, and which span store replaced the costly proprietary agent they ran before?
gold_answer: OpenTelemetry was adopted for instrumentation with Grafana Tempo as the trace backend, replacing custom Datadog APM agents.
evidence_prose: 2025-01-08 — Platform Q1 kickoff notes (Elena): Datadog APM bill hit $22k in December — unacceptable. Decision finalized before holidays: rip out dd-trace-py, instrument everything with the otel-sdk, ship spans to Tempo in our existing Grafana Cloud org. Migration target: all six services on OTel by end of January. Carla owns the embedding service leg; Dmitri owns the router + reranker.

question: What caused the ranking service to go down in mid-2024 and how did the team resolve it?
gold_answer: Memory leak in the cross-encoder tokenizer's batch processing; fixed by capping batch size to 32 and adding explicit cleanup after each request.
evidence_prose:
2024-08-14 · Search on-call · Dmitri
Post-mortem closed. Heap growth traced to cross-encoder token buffer accumulation — each inference pass held references that GC wasn't clearing between requests under concurrent load. Short-term: hard ceiling at 32 inputs per pass, destructor call added in the finally block. P50 latency recovered within 10 min of deploy. Longer-term mitigation (token buffer pooling) tracked in HELIX-2201.

question: Why did customers lose access to paid tiers overnight in late 2024 and what was the immediate fix?
gold_answer: Stripe webhook signing secret was rotated without updating HELIX's secrets store; all renewal events were rejected. Fix was syncing the new secret into the secrets store.
evidence_prose:
2024-11-07 · Billing SRE · Elena
Incident duration 6h 22m. Payment processor rotated the endpoint signing credential on their side as part of a quarterly key cycle — our vault copy was stale. Every inbound subscription lifecycle event returned 403 at our webhook handler, so renewals silently dropped and the entitlement worker downgraded accounts. Hotfix: synced new value into Vault, reprocessed the 14-hour backlog from the processor's event log. Action item: automate secret-rotation parity checks (HELIX-2389).

question: Why did the early-2025 data import fail in a way where records looked successfully saved but carried no usable meaning for retrieval?
gold_answer: The object store returned HTTP 200 with empty bodies during maintenance, which the pipeline misread as valid empty documents instead of errors, producing empty embedding vectors.
evidence_prose:
2025-01-19 · Platform on-call · Ben
Root cause confirmed. Provider maintenance caused their gateway to emit 200 responses with zero-byte payloads rather than 503. Our fetch layer checked only HTTP status; content-length validation was absent. Downstream, the embedding service faithfully encoded empty strings and wrote zero-norm vectors to the index. Queries during the 3h window returned degraded results silently. Fix shipped same day: added payload length assertion before enqueue, plus alert on mean vector norm dropping below threshold.

question: What caused partial corruption of the search index after the network event in autumn 2024?
gold_answer: Asymmetric network partition healing caused dual-leader split-brain on four shards, producing duplicate write acknowledgments and corrupted index segments.
evidence_prose:
2024-09-03 · Platform · Carla
Post-mortem merged. Partition lasted ~80s; when the link recovered, the lease-renewal heartbeat arrived at both replicas within the same TTL window — both asserted primary status. Concurrent segment compaction on shards 7, 12, 19, 23 wrote conflicting manifests. Recovery: forced leader stepdown via admin API, replayed WAL from last clean checkpoint. Four shards replayed ~2h of writes. Lease TTL tightened from 30s→10s and we added a fencing token check on every compaction lock (HELIX-1977).

question: When did one client's private search hits get exposed to unrelated accounts, and what was the underlying technical mistake?
gold_answer: During a blue-green deployment in Q2'24, the query router config pointed all tenants' queries to one tenant's dedicated index pool for 22 minutes, exposing results across tenants.
evidence_prose:
2024-05-30 · Search · Ana
SEV-1 closed, legal notified per breach protocol. Blue-green cutover script swapped the router's index-pool mapping table but a template rendering bug left the tenant-routing field hardcoded to pool-id 'enterprise-0014' across all rows. Duration: 22 min before on-call caught anomalous hit-rate spike on that pool. Affected: 9 tenants received result sets from enterprise-0014's corpus. Immediate fix: rolled router config, invalidated all active query sessions. Long-term: tenant-pool mapping now rendered per-row with a diff assertion step before any cutover proceeds (HELIX-1803).

question: What was behind the silent accuracy drop in the vector-generation service in early 2025 that surfaced only under heavy traffic?
gold_answer: A missing per-thread CUDA context initialization caused workers to share a single context, producing race conditions and NaN embedding vectors under concurrent load.
evidence_prose:
2025-02-11 · Search on-call · Dmitri
Trace finally closed. The GPU context was initialized once in the main process and inherited by forked workers — fine at low concurrency, but under the load spike from Growth's new bulk-ingest campaign, concurrent kernel dispatches from separate threads collided inside the shared context. Output tensors contained NaN at a rate correlated with request parallelism. Vectors were stored without NaN checks, so the corruption was invisible until eval harness nDCG dropped 0.12 points overnight. Fix: moved context init into each worker's startup hook, added embedding norm assertion before index write (HELIX-2541).

question: How did the team discover that quality benchmarks had been giving false-green results for a month and a half in mid-2024?
gold_answer: A cron job refreshing the golden eval dataset had been failing silently due to expired credentials for six weeks; benchmarks ran against stale data and missed real regressions.
evidence_prose:
2024-06-18 · Eval harness · Ana
RCA done. The nightly dataset sync job began failing 2024-05-05 when the read token for the annotation bucket expired; no alert was wired to that job's exit code. Harness kept running against the May 4th snapshot. Two model updates (v2.3, v2.4) were shipped with green evals that would have shown -0.07 nDCG on current annotations. Caught only when a new engineer ran a manual end-to-end check. Actions: credential expiry alerts (HELIX-1865), eval dataset manifest hash logged and diffed each run (HELIX-1866).

question: Why were European users unable to log into the product for several hours in autumn 2024?
gold_answer: A CDN edge rule mis-routed EU users' authenticated traffic to the US origin, where their session tokens were rejected because JWT signing keys are scoped per region.
evidence_prose:
2024-10-09 · Growth / SRE · Elena
Post-mortem done. Edge rule deployed as part of GDPR data-residency work used a wildcard that matched /app/* before the more-specific /app/eu/* route. All dashboard traffic landed on us-east-1 origin. Session validation there rejected tokens because Frankfurt's signing key is not in the US keyring. Users saw HTTP 401 loops. Duration: 4h 11m. Fix: corrected rule specificity order, added synthetic canary that authenticates from EU probe and asserts sub-100ms origin RTT to fra-01.

question: Why did response times slowly worsen on the largest customers' data partitions in spring 2025 with no software release involved?
gold_answer: The compaction job inherited a stale systemd fd limit, causing it to abort silently on shards over 40GB; unmerged tombstones accumulated, inflating per-query scan cost.
evidence_prose:
2025-03-06 · Platform · Carla
Root cause identified. systemd unit for `helix-compactor` still had LimitNOFILE=1024 from the pre-migration config; updated limit (65536) was in the Ansible template but the unit was never reloaded after the infra move. Shards above ~40GB hit the fd ceiling mid-compaction, job exited 0 (misleading), segment manifest was left with unmerged delete markers. Scan cost on affected shards grew linearly with tombstone count — explains the 3-week p99 latency creep on enterprise tenants. Fix: reloaded unit, ran forced compaction sweep, added post-compaction tombstone-ratio assertion to alert before next cycle (HELIX-2698).

question: What broke processing of non-English files in summer 2024 right after a routine library-cleanup change?
gold_answer: A PDF parsing library was unpinned and auto-upgraded; the newer version changed text extraction encoding from UTF-8 to Latin-1, crashing the embedding service on non-ASCII characters.
evidence_prose:
2024-08-27 · Platform / Search · Ben
Cause confirmed. HELIX-1901 (dependency hygiene) removed the version cap on `pdfminer-six` to get a security patch; 20.25.0 changed the default output codec. Ingestion workers began throwing UnicodeDecodeError on any document with accented characters or CJK content — roughly 18% of the enterprise corpus. Pipeline silently dropped those jobs after 3 retry exhaustions. Embedding throughput drop wasn't caught until a tenant filed a ticket about missing Japanese-language docs. Fix: pinned library to last known-good, added charset normalization step before enqueue. Long-term: integration test suite now includes a multilingual fixture set (HELIX-1944).

question: How many documents at a time does the embedding worker process in a single forward pass?
evidence_prose: 2024-03-12 — Platform standup: Carla landed the embed-service tuning PR. Tried 128 and 256; both tanked GPU util (PCIe was the ceiling, not compute). Settled on 64 per batch. Perf numbers in the PR description.

question: What build-time exploration factor is used when constructing the approximate nearest-neighbor graph?
evidence_prose: 2024-05-29 — Search RFC-07 outcome: we ran the recall sweep last week. At 128 the harness flagged recall@10 dipping under target (0.92 floor). Bumped to 200; build is 40% slower but recall holds at 0.95. Merging with 200 as the permanent default.

question: At what daily request volume does a non-paying customer start receiving rate-limit errors?
gold_answer: 10,000 API calls per day
evidence_prose: 2025-01-08 — Growth standup: Elena confirmed the free-plan ceiling is live in prod. Set at 10 k/day; anything over returns 429. We saw one tenant hit it on launch day — working as intended. Billing-service config commit b3f9a1.

question: How many sub-quantizer segments are used in the compressed representation of stored vectors?
evidence_prose: 2024-10-22 — Platform RFC-14 outcome: tested PQ32, PQ64, PQ96, PQ128 against the recall harness on the production snapshot. PQ96 was the elbow — going to 128 gave less than 0.2% lift. Memory footprint at 96 is 3× smaller than float32. Approved; merging.

question: Who is currently on the hook for waking up to production pages across the entire stack?
gold_answer: A unified SRE rotation covering all components (Search, Platform, Growth, Billing)
evidence_prose: 2025-01-07 | SRE handoff email — as of Monday the dedicated SRE pool owns the pager for all services; Growth and Billing runbooks have been migrated into PagerDuty under the HELIX-SRE schedule. The Search/Platform split schedule is deactivated. Escalation paths unchanged: P0 wakes Dmitri.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-08-19 | On-call RFC-11 outcome — Search-only rotation was a single point of failure during the index outage; Platform engineers added to the rotation starting Q3 on-call cycle; Growth and Billing remain self-on-call for now.

question: Which group ended up responsible for the cross-encoder scoring layer after it was pulled out of the retrieval stack?
gold_answer: Platform team
evidence_prose:
2024-07-11 — Search standup: shipping reranker v1 behind feature flag; Carla owns the rollout. Elena flagged latency tail risk, asked if this should live in infra layer long-term.

2024-09-03 — Platform design doc (reranker-as-service RFC): decision to absorb reranker into Platform shared infra, effective this sprint. Carla transitioning ownership to Dmitri. Rationale: three separate teams were each planning their own re-scoring wrappers.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-09-03 — Platform design doc (reranker-as-service RFC): decision to absorb reranker into Platform shared infra, effective this sprint. Carla transitioning ownership to Dmitri. Rationale: three separate teams were each planning their own re-scoring wrappers.

question: After the move to a wider vector representation, what corpus-wide rebuild did the storage tier have to perform?
gold_answer: A full re-index of the vector store was required.
evidence_prose:
2024-11-19 — Embedding service RFC v2: Ben proposes migrating from all-mpnet-base-v2 (768-d) to text-embedding-3-large (1536-d); cites 6-point NDCG lift on internal eval set. Flagged: any dimension change is a hard incompatibility — index layer cannot reuse existing vectors.

2025-01-14 — Platform on-call log: full re-index job kicked off at 02:00 UTC, estimated 11 hours. Triggered by embedding model cutover that shipped last Thursday. Vector store held ~2.1B vectors; job completed 13:47 UTC with 0 drops.
second_evidence_prose: 2025-01-14 — Platform on-call log: full re-index job kicked off at 02:00 UTC, estimated 11 hours. Triggered by embedding model cutover that shipped last Thursday. Vector store held ~2.1B vectors; job completed 13:47 UTC with 0 drops.

question: Who authored the commit that triggered the dropped-records outage, and had that same engineer also designed the intake subsystem originally?
gold_answer: Ben made the change that caused the incident; Ana originally architected the ingestion pipeline — they are different people.
evidence_prose:
2024-03-05 — Ingestion design doc v1 (author: Ana): outlines the chunking strategy, idempotency keys, and dead-letter queue. Ana signs off as DRI.

2024-06-28 — Post-mortem (P0, ingestion data loss): root cause — offset commit logic modified in PR #1847 (author: Ben) dropped chunks when batch size exceeded 512. Ana's original idempotency design would have caught this, but PR #1847 bypassed the dedup path. Ana had rotated to Growth in April; Ben inherited the service without a formal handoff doc.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-06-28 — Post-mortem (P0, ingestion data loss): root cause — offset commit logic modified in PR #1847 (author: Ben) dropped chunks when batch size exceeded 512. Ana's original idempotency design would have caught this, but PR #1847 bypassed the dedup path. Ana had rotated to Growth in April; Ben inherited the service without a formal handoff doc.

question: What was the first live experiment run through the routing layer's split-testing mechanism, and did it become permanent?
gold_answer: The synonym expansion experiment (Growth team) was the first live A/B experiment; it became the default after a 9% CTR lift.
evidence_prose:
2024-08-22 — Query router RFC: adding A/B traffic-split capability; immediate consumer is Growth's upcoming lexical-expansion trial. Elena implemented the framework; Growth team is the first tenant. Launch target: week of Sep 9.

2024-10-01 — Growth standup: synonym-expansion experiment concluded, 9.1% CTR uplift, p<0.01 on 14-day window. Ana recommending full rollout. Dmitri confirmed router config to flip default next deploy.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-10-01 — Growth standup: synonym-expansion experiment concluded, 9.1% CTR uplift, p<0.01 on 14-day window. Ana recommending full rollout. Dmitri confirmed router config to flip default next deploy.

question: How did the assessment framework's coverage change once the re-ranking stage joined the pipeline?
gold_answer: The eval harness was extended from retrieval-precision-only to also include end-to-end answer faithfulness judged by an LLM.
evidence_prose:
2024-02-14 — Eval harness kickoff notes: scope is retrieval metrics only — P@5, NDCG@10. Carla is DRI. Faithfulness / generation quality explicitly deferred; no LLM judge budget approved yet.

2024-10-17 — Eval harness changelog (Carla): added faithfulness_score pipeline stage; uses Claude Haiku as judge, prompt template in eval/prompts/faithfulness_v1.txt. Triggered by reranker integration — Search asked whether re-scored results actually improve downstream answer quality, not just rank correlation.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-10-17 — Eval harness changelog (Carla): added faithfulness_score pipeline stage; uses Claude Haiku as judge, prompt template in eval/prompts/faithfulness_v1.txt. Triggered by reranker integration — Search asked whether re-scored results actually improve downstream answer quality, not just rank correlation.

question: After the shift to usage-based pricing, which two internal services had to start emitting metered events?
gold_answer: The ingestion pipeline and query router both required new metering instrumentation to support consumption-based billing.
evidence_prose:
2024-10-08 — Growth pricing RFC (Ana, author): proposes moving from per-seat to usage-based model — charge per 1K vectors ingested and per 1K queries routed. Flagged instrumentation gaps: ingestion pipeline has no per-tenant vector counter; query router logs requests but doesn't emit billable events to the billing service.

2024-11-30 — Billing service deploy notes: v2.4.0 ships consumption metering. Ingestion pipeline PR #2201 (Ben) and query router PR #2209 (Elena) both merged this week; each emits `billing.event` to the metering bus. Seat-based plans deprecated for new signups.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-11-30 — Billing service deploy notes: v2.4.0 ships consumption metering. Ingestion pipeline PR #2201 (Ben) and query router PR #2209 (Elena) both merged this week; each emits `billing.event` to the metering bus. Seat-based plans deprecated for new signups.

question: Did the graph-based approximate nearest-neighbor structure stay in service across every customer tier following the slowdown early in 2025?
gold_answer: No — high-cardinality tenants were migrated to IVF-PQ after the latency regression; HNSW was not retained for that segment.
evidence_prose:
2024-01-09 — Vector index store design doc (Dmitri): selecting HNSW as the primary index structure. Rationale: strong recall at low-latency for expected corpus sizes; hnswlib bindings already in-house. IVF-PQ noted as a future option for very large shards.

2025-02-18 — SRE post-mortem (p95 latency regression, Jan 31): HNSW graph traversal degrades super-linearly past ~50M vectors per shard; three enterprise tenants breached that threshold in January. Resolution: IVF-PQ index rebuilt for tenants >20M vectors; HNSW retained for standard tier. Dmitri and Elena co-owned the migration.
second_evidence_prose: 2025-02-18 — SRE post-mortem (p95 latency regression, Jan 31): HNSW graph traversal degrades super-linearly past ~50M vectors per shard; three enterprise tenants breached that threshold in January. Resolution: IVF-PQ index rebuilt for tenants >20M vectors; HNSW retained for standard tier. Dmitri and Elena co-owned the migration.

question: When the customer-facing surface added its usage-inspection view, did the team stand up fresh data plumbing or reuse a store already running for operations?
gold_answer: They repurposed an existing ClickHouse table that Platform had already provisioned for SRE use — no new data infrastructure was built.
evidence_prose:
2024-07-30 — Growth feature request (Ana): dashboard needs a query analytics panel — tenants want to see top queries, latency distribution, zero-result rate. No current user-facing analytics surface exists.

2024-11-05 — Web dashboard deploy notes (v3.1): ships QueryAnalyticsPanel. Backend reads from `sre.query_events` ClickHouse table — same table Platform stood up in March for on-call dashboards. Ben added tenant-scoped read path; no new ingestion pipeline needed. Growth signed off on the column subset exposed to customers.
second_evidence_prose: 2024-11-05 — Web dashboard deploy notes (v3.1): ships QueryAnalyticsPanel. Backend reads from `sre.query_events` ClickHouse table — same table Platform stood up in March for on-call dashboards. Ben added tenant-scoped read path; no new ingestion pipeline needed. Growth signed off on the column subset exposed to customers.
